coredata.dc:title: A cascade FRET photosensitizer that enhances photodynamic therapy for ocular melanoma
coredata.dc:creator: Ruan, Jing
Li, Fang
Tian, Hao
Yu, Jie
Deng, Hongpei
Ge, Shengfang
Leong, Kam W.
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) relies on the generation of cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2) from an otherwise nontoxic photosensitizer accumulated in tumor tissue to treat cancer. However, PDT of uveal melanoma, the most common adult intraocular cancer, using potent porphyrin derivatives as photosensitizers has shown limited therapeutic efficacy due to low bioavailability and 1O2 yield. Here, considering the uveal melanoma microenvironment of endogenous melanin, which has a high absorption between 300 nm and 500 nm, we design a carbon dot-based photosensitizer that uses multiple different pathways to generate 1O2. We conjugated gadolinium (Gd)-doped carbon dots (Gd@Cdots) with the porphyrin-derivatives chlorin e6 (Ce6) and verteporfin (Ver). The resulting nanoparticle (Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver) generates 1O2 under UV irradiation mainly in cascade FRET from Gd@Cdots to Ce6 and then to Ver; the photoactivated Ver, in turn, transfers its energy to 3O2 to generate 1O2. In human uveal melanoma MUM-2B cells in vitro, Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver generated 7.1- and 2.4-fold higher production of hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide anions (O2 -·) following UV irradiation than Gd@Cdots, indicating greater induction of tumor cell apoptosis. In an orthotopic mouse model of uveal melanoma, PDT with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver showed the same trend of superior potency in tumor growth inhibition. This photosensitizer design improves the PDT efficacy of uveal melanoma by enhancing the production of 1O2 and should be of broad utility to other PDT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uveal melanoma
Photodynamic therapy (PDT)
Photosensitizer
Porphyrin derivatives
Cascade FRET
Singlet oxygen (1O2)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Various reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver
Cell viability, migration, and apoptosis after PDT treatment
PDT treatment in intraocular uveal melanoma model
Biosafety assessment of nanoparticles in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of cascade FRET nanoparticles. a) Synthesis of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver. b) TEM of Gd@Cdots (2–3 nm diameter). Scale bar, 20 nm. c) TEM of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver nanoparticles (avg. diameter ∼40 nm). Scale bar, 50 nm. d) HRTEM of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver nanoparticles. Scale bar, 10 nm. e) Absorption spectra of photosensitizers and Gd@Cdots. f) Absorption spectra of Ce6 and Ver, and emission spectrum of Gd@Cdots. g) Absorption spectrum of Ver, and emission spectrum of Gd@Cdots/Ce6.

Singlet oxygen and ROS generation. a) Schematic of 1O2 production by UV-irradiated Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver via cascade FRET followed by TET. b) 1O2 generation pathways under UV irradiation. c) Time-dependent change in DPBF absorption spectrum, indicating increased generation of 1O2 by Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver. d) Quantification of 1O2 production versus irradiation time for various modified Gd@Cdots by DPBF assay. A larger absolute value of Δ(OD415 nm) indicates greater 1O2 concentration. e, f) Quantification of·OH and O2 -· in Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver solution under different irradiation time via TMB and DHR123 indicator.

Intracellular localization of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver and intracellular ROS levels. a) Confocal microscopy images of MUM-2B cells. Cells were stained with DAPI (blue) and F-actin (green); nanoparticle emission appears red under excitation at 647 nm. Overlapping nanoparticle and DAPI emission (purple in merged images) indicates localization of nanoparticles in the nucleus. b,c) Analysis of relative intracellular ROS level in cells treated with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver with and without UV irradiation (PDT/No PDT). (d, e) The cellular·OH and O2 -·level after treatment Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver with or without UV irradiation (PDT/No PDT).

MUM-2B and OCM1 uveal melanoma cell phenotypes after PDT with various photosensitizers. a) Heat map of cell proliferation results for MUM-2B and OCM1 cells. Red indicates low cell density; blue indicates high cell density. b) Trypan blue staining of MUM-2B cells after treatment with PDT. c) Percent apoptotic cells following treatment with different photosensitizers with and without irradiation (PDT). d) Flow cytometry analysis of cell apoptosis rate following different treatment.

Induction of tumor cell apoptosis by PDT with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver. a) Western blotting of apoptosis-related, mitochondrial, and reference proteins following different cell treatments. b) Relative expression levels of mitochondrial proteins. c) Relative expression levels of apoptosis-related proteins. d) Schematic of induction of tumor cell apoptosis by Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver.

Therapeutic efficacy of PDT with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in subcutaneous and intraocular tumor models. a) Experimental design and timeline. b) Biodistribution of Gd@Cdots, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in subcutaneous tumor model at 4 h and 24 h after IV injection. c) Tumor volume in mice after PDT. d) Tumor weight in mice after PDT. e) IVIS imaging of intraocular tumor mice after IV injection of nanoparticles 24 h. f) Change in bioluminescence in region of interest (ROI) of intraocular tumor mice after PDT. g) Tumor eye weight after PDT. h) Representative photographs, H&E and TUNEL staining images of tumor-bearing eyes from different treatment groups at the end of PDT.

A cascade FRET photosensitizer that yields greater 1O2 production to enhance PDT of uveal melanoma. Gadolinium (Gd)-doped carbon dots (Cdots) conjugated with chlorin e6 (Ce6) and verteporfin (Ver) yielded photosensitizing cascade FRET nanoparticles (D/A1/A2 = Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver). These nanoparticles generate great singlet oxygen (1O2), 1O2 is easily quenched and then cascaded produce amounts of·OH at acidic microenvironment in photodynamic therapy (PDT) of uveal melanoma in orthotopic tumor-bearing mice.

head.ce:title: A cascade FRET photosensitizer that enhances photodynamic therapy for ocular melanoma

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft，Funding acquisition，Visualization

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Ophthalmology, Shanghai Ninth People’s Hospital, Shanghai JiaoTong University School of Medicine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) relies on the generation of cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2) from an otherwise nontoxic photosensitizer accumulated in tumor tissue to treat cancer. However, PDT of uveal melanoma, the most common adult intraocular cancer, using potent porphyrin derivatives as photosensitizers has shown limited therapeutic efficacy due to low bioavailability and 1O2 yield. Here, considering the uveal melanoma microenvironment of endogenous melanin, which has a high absorption between 300 nm and 500 nm, we design a carbon dot-based photosensitizer that uses multiple different pathways to generate 1O2. We conjugated gadolinium (Gd)-doped carbon dots (Gd@Cdots) with the porphyrin-derivatives chlorin e6 (Ce6) and verteporfin (Ver). The resulting nanoparticle (Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver) generates 1O2 under UV irradiation mainly in cascade FRET from Gd@Cdots to Ce6 and then to Ver; the photoactivated Ver, in turn, transfers its energy to 3O2 to generate 1O2. In human uveal melanoma MUM-2B cells in vitro, Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver generated 7.1- and 2.4-fold higher production of hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide anions (O2 -·) following UV irradiation than Gd@Cdots, indicating greater induction of tumor cell apoptosis. In an orthotopic mouse model of uveal melanoma, PDT with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver showed the same trend of superior potency in tumor growth inhibition. This photosensitizer design improves the PDT efficacy of uveal melanoma by enhancing the production of 1O2 and should be of broad utility to other PDT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uveal melanoma is the most common intraocular malignant tumor in adults and can be caused by inherited genetic mutations or by exposure of melanocytes to ultraviolet light and oxidative stress [1]. Oxidative stress results from an imbalance between the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS; i.e., oxygen free radicals) and antioxidants. ROS are generated in melanocytes during melanin synthesis in the presence of external irradiation. ROS is a double-edged sword: while excess ROS can be tumorigenic [2], ROS can also be wielded to disrupt the redox balance in cancer cells and induce cancer cell apoptosis via DNA damage and lipid peroxidation [3]. ROS-based therapeutic strategies have been developed to treat cancers including uveal melanoma [4,5]; these strategies include plaque brachytherapy with different radioisotopes and proton beam therapy [6–8]. However, these ROS-based treatments have been limited by nonspecific toxicity and have resulted in postoperative complications such as vision impairment, dry eye, cataracts, keratitis, and scleritis [9,10].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) was developed to address this nonspecificity. PDT is an FDA-approved anticancer modality in which external light is used to activate an otherwise nontoxic photosensitizer (PS) accumulated in tumor tissue to generate cytotoxic ROS through energy or electron transfer [11,12]. However, PDT for uveal melanoma has been limited by the low yield of singlet oxygen (1O2) produced by the PS [13–15]. Novel PS have been developed to improve 1O2 production efficiency, including PS with better tumor selectivity (greater accumulation in tumors) and PS activated by longer wavelengths of light (e.g. near-infrared), which have greater tissue penetration [16–18]. Even though considering the uveal melanoma microenvironment of endogenous melanin, which is produced by melanocytes and has a high absorption between 300 nm and 500 nm, porphyrin derivatives such as chlorin e6 (Ce6) and verteporfin (Ver) (with a peak absorption at 420 nm) were developed to be used as PS treat uveal melanoma [14,19]. However, self-quenching, poor biodistribution and low cellular uptake have limited the PDT efficiency[20]. These issues were addressed by using carbon dots (Cdots) conjugated with photosensitizing agents [21–23], because Cdots exhibit excellent photostability, water solubility, biocompatibility, and wide excitation wavelength range. It has been widely used as medical imaging contrast agents, drug carriers, and chemoradiotherapy sensitizers [24]. However, the short life time of 1O2 is still the main reason to cause PDT low efficiency, because it can be easily quenched and then yield various of ROS. It is worth noting that amounts of ROS could play synergetic roles in treating cancer with 1O2 ( Schematic 1).

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) can be used to transfer the excitation energy of one fluorophore (the donor) to another fluorophore (the acceptor) within 10 nm; in some cases, the excited acceptor can transfer its energy to 3O2 to induce 1O2 generation. Cascade FRET can be used to couple multiple donor-acceptor pairs [25,26], having the advantages of relatively low laser intensity with good biosafety, high ROS yield with efficient PDT, and precise tumor theranostic. Here we conjugated the porphyrin-derivative photosensitizers Ce6 and Ver to Gd-doped Cdots to create a cascade FRET photosensitizer (D/A1/A2 = Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver, Schematic 1) that can generate 1O2 under UV irradiation in three possible ways: (i) cascade FRET from Gd@Cdots to Ce6 to Ver; (ii) FRET between Gd@Cdots and Ce6; and (iii) FRET between Gd@Cdots and Ver; photoactivated Ver and Ce6, in turn, transfer their energy to 3O2 to generate 1O2. We compare the performance of this photosensitizer with that of other photosensitizers—Ce6, Ver, Gd@Cdots without Ce6 and Ver, and Gd@Cdots/Ce6—in terms of 1O2 / ROS production, inhibition of tumor cell growth and migration, and induction of apoptosis in vitro, and PDT therapeutic efficacy in an orthotopic tumor-bearing mouse model of uveal melanoma.

Chlorin e6 (Ce6) was purchased from Frontier Scientific Inc. (UT, USA) and verteporfin (Ver) was purchased from Sellek (Shanghai, China). Dihydrorhodamin123 (DHR123) and Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Dihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), phalloidin-FITC, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), and anti-β-actin antibody were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). MitoSOX Red was purchased from Invitrogen (CA, USA). CCK-8 solution was purchased from Dojindo (Japan). DMEM medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and penicillin-streptomycin solution were obtained from Gibco (New York, USA). Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis detection kits were purchased from BD Biosciences (USA). Anti-Bax, anti-Bcl-xl, anti-cyto-C, anti-H2AX, anti-Fas, anti-Caspase-3, and anti-cleaved caspase-3 antibodies were obtained from Cell Signaling (MA, USA). The human ocular melanoma cell lines MUM-2B and OCM1 were provided by Dr. John F. Marshall (Tumor Biology Laboratory, Cancer Research UK Clinical Center, John Vane Science Centre, London, UK).

Gd@Cdots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method as we have described previously[27]. Briefly, 0.25 g Trp, 300 mg GdCl3.6H2O, and 0.5 g glucose were mixed into 30 mL water solution under vigorous stirring. After the mixture became clear, the solution was autoclaved at 200 °C for 20 min. The resulting dark brown product was cooled to room temperature and centrifugated at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove agglomerated particles. The supernatant was dialyzed (MSCO:100KD) in ddH2O for 24 h to obtain purified Gd@Cdots. The Gd3+ mass ratio in Gd@Cdots was determined by ICP-MS and calculated as follows (1):
(1) Gd3+ mass ratio (%) = CGd/CNP *100%,
where CGd is the concentration of Gd3+ in Gd@Cdots nanoparticle and CNP is the concentration of Gd@Cdots nanoparticle.

Ce6 and Ver were chemically conjugated to Gd@Cdots via amidation. Ce6 and Ver were firstly dissolved in 1 mL pH5.0 ddH2O at 1 mM each, 10 mM EDC and 2.5 mM NHS were added into the above mixture for activation 1 h, after that the mixture pH was adjusted to 7.0 and 1 mL of 1 mg/mL Gd@Cdots were added to the mixture and allowed to react for 0.5 h. The resulting Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver were purified with a 1000 kDa ultrafilter at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. Ce6 and Ver conjugation efficiency were quantified based on UV–vis absorbance according to Eq. (2) [23]:
(2) Conjugating rate (%) = (1- C1/C0) *100
Where C1 is the supernatant Ce6 or Ver concentration calculated from the standard curves of absorbance at 402 nm (Ce6) and 690 nm (Ver). C0 is the primary concentration of Ce6 or Ver.

The morphology and size of the Gd@Cdots and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver were characterized by TEM on a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of nanomaterials were measured with a Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer and Hitachi FL-4600 spectrofluorimeter. Zeta potentials were measured with a Zetasizer ZSU3200 (Malvern Panalytical). The chemical composition of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was determined by XPS using an RBD-upgraded PHI-5000C ESCA system (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) with Mg Kα radiation (hν=1253.6 eV) at 250 W and 14.0 kV with a 54° detection angle.

Different amounts of Ce6 and Ver (0, 8, 40, 200, 1000 μM per drug) were chemically conjugated to Gd@Cdots, then the purified Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was scanned PL spectrum at 340 nm excitation. The PL intensity peak within the emission wavelength range from 350 nm to 650 nm was recorded and was calculated the FRET efficiency according to the Eq. (3) as reported [28]:
(3) FRET efficiency (%) = (1- FD/FDA) *100
Where FD is the PL intensity of Gd@Cdots in the absence of an acceptor, FDA is the corresponding PL intensities of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in the presence of the acceptor (Ce6/Ver) at the same donor (Gd@Cdots) concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Various reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of cells, a laser (405 nm, 10 mW/cm2) was used as the excitation light in singlet oxygen (1O2) generation tests of various nanoparticles dispersed in various solvents. 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used to measure 1O2 production [18], whereas dihydrorhodamin123 (DHR123) and tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was used for the superoxide anions (O2-·) and hydroxyl radical (·OH), respectively [29]. In a typical experiment, 10 µM Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, or Gd@Cdots was separately suspended in 1 mL dichloromethane containing different indicators, the changes in DPBF absorption, DHR123 fluorescent intensities, and TMB absorbances were measured with UV-Vis spectrophotometer and microplate reader after irradiation for 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 8 min

In the presence of cells, total ROS was systematically evaluated at intercellular and mitochondrial levels [30]. Briefly, MUM-2B cells were plated in 6-well plates and grown to proper density, the cells were starved with serum-free media overnight and then were incubated with nanoparticles for 4 h, following, the intracellular ROS was detected after cells were incubated with DCFH-DA (Sigma) for 20 min and treated with PDT through FACS Calibur flow cytometer (BD Biosciences) or microplate reader, similarly, the mitochondrial ROS was performed after cells were incubated with 5 μM of MitoSOX Red (Invitrogen) for 10 min via flow cytometer. The results were analyzed using FlowJo software. The data were normalized to the mean fluorescence intensity of the NC group without PDT treatment.

The intercellular ROS including O2 - and·OH were separately analyzed using the specific fluorescent indicators. The cells were preincubated in a serum-free medium for 24 h and cocultured with nanomaterials for 4 h. The culture medium was then changed to 1 μM DHR123 and TMB for 20 min, after that the cells were irradiated by 405 nm laser light (10 mW/cm2, 1 min) and the cellular fluorescence intensity or absorbance was determined. The data was normalized to the mean fluorescence intensity or absorbance of the NC group without PDT treatment.

MUM-2B cells were incubated with 50 µg/mL of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in 1 mL of culture media for 2 h, the cells were washed twice with PBS and were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and were treated with phalloidin (green) and DAPI (blue) for 0.5 h at 37 °C. The subcellular localization of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was analyzed by imaging with an Olympus X71 fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability, migration, and apoptosis after PDT treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Cell viability was evaluated via trypan blue staining. MUM-2B, OCM1, RPE19, or HUVEC cells were seeded in six-well plates and were serum-starved overnight. The cells were then treated with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, Gd@Cdots, or with buffer only (NC), with or without UV irradiation as follows. 10 µL of 50 μg/mL nanoparticles were added to the culture medium and the cells were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. The medium was then replaced with fresh media and the cells were irradiated at 405 nm with laser light at 10 mW/cm2 for 1 min. Cell aliquots were mixed with trypan blue and images were acquired. Cell viability was evaluated by CCK8 assay as follows. MUM-2B cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 3 × 103 cells per well and were treated with nanoparticles and UV irradiation. CCK-8 solution (Dojindo, Japan) was added to the medium and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured.

To evaluate cell migration, MUM-2B cells were treated with nanoparticles and UV irradiation, and were collected and seeded into the upper chamber of a 24-well transwell system (Corning, USA) in DMEM medium with 1% FBS. After culturing for 24 h, the migrated cells in the upper chamber were stained with crystal violet.

Cell apoptosis was evaluated by FACS. MUM-2B or OCM1 cells were plated in 6-cm dishes and were serum-starved overnight. After culturing overnight with nanoparticles, some cells were irradiated and some cells were not irradiated. Immediately after treatment, cells were collected and apoptotic cells were detected with an Annexin V-FITC/PI Apoptosis Detection Kit I (BD Biosciences, USA). Data were analyzed with FlowJo software.

MUM-2B cells were treated with nanoparticles and UV irradiation (LED, 10 mW/cm2, 20 s) for 24 h. Proteins were harvested and lysed. Proteins were resolved using SDS-PAGE and were transferred to a PVDF membrane (Millipore, USA). The membrane was blocked in 5% BSA and incubated with anti-Bax (1:500), anti-Bcl-xl (1:500), anti-cyto-C (1:500), anti-caspase3 (1:1000), anti-Fas (1:1000), anti-H2AX (1:1000), and anti-β-actin (1:5000) antibodies overnight at 4 °C. Subsequently, the membrane was washed and incubated with secondary antibodies. HRP-conjugated anti-rabbit IgG (1:1000, Invitrogen, CA, USA) was used as a secondary antibody. Protein expression was visualized using an Odyssey image scanner.

All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Research Committee of Shanghai Ninth People’s Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine. Subcutaneous ocular melanoma models were constructed with 1 × 106 bioluminescent MUM-2B cells using 4-week-old nude mice. Mice were divided into the following groups: Gd@Cdots, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver, and were IV injected with 100 µL of 1 mg/mL of nanoparticles on days 4, 12, 20, and 28, and were subjected to photodynamic therapy at 24 h after injection. To evaluate nanoparticle biodistributions, mice were imaged by IVIS at 0, 4, and 24 h after the first injection, and were sacrificed and major organs were collected for ex vivo imaging. The fluorescence images were captured under 430 nm excitation and 680 nm emission. Tumor volume was recorded on days 0, 4, 8, 12, and 16. Tumor weights were measured after harvesting subcutaneous tumor tissues on day 28 and the quantification of nanoparticles in main tissues was determined by ICP-AES (Thermo Fisher, iCAP 7400).

The T1-weighted relaxivity of Gd@Cdots was obtained on a 3.0T MRI instrument (Magnetom Verio; Siemens, Germany). The following samples were dispersed in ddH2O: MRI contrast agent, Gd@Cdots, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver, Ce6, Ver, Ce6 mixture with Ver (Ce6/Ver), PBS-only. T1-weighted images were acquired using the following parameters: TR= 600 ms, TE= 10 ms, field-of-view (FOV)= 100 × 100 mm2, slice= 2.5, slice thickness= 3.5 mm, and image matrix= 512 × 512. Relaxivity values of r1 were calculated by fitting the 1/T1 relaxation time to the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver concentrations. In the in vivo MRI study, 100 µL of 1 mg/mL of nanoparticles were IV injected into subcutaneous tumor-bearing mice, and sagittal and coronal T1-weighted MR images were acquired at 0 min, 4 h, and 24 h post nanoparticle injection. Images were acquired using the following parameters: TR/TE= 800/11 ms, FOV= 100 × 100 mm2, matrix size= 256 × 256 cm, slice= 2, and thickness= 3.0 mm. Relative contrast signals in tumor sites were normalized to the Ce6–0 h group value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PDT treatment in intraocular uveal melanoma model
ce:section.ce:para: In situ tumor models were constructed via intraocular injection of bioluminescent MUM-2B cells. Intraocular tumors were confirmed after 3 weeks by IVIS imaging. Mice were randomly divided into Gd@Cdots, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver groups, nanoparticles were injected through the caudal vein, tumors were irradiated every week, and were imaged with an IVIS system 24 h after injection. Bioluminescent signals in ROIs around tumors were analyzed in total scaled photons/cm2/s weekly. All mice were sacrificed and tumor eyes were harvested and weighed to evaluate tumor size. Tumor eyes were then fixed in 10% formaldehyde solution for H&E and TUNEL staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety assessment of nanoparticles in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy ICR mice were used for in vivo biosafety assessment. The mice were randomly divided into 3 groups (PBS, Gd@Cdots, and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver) with 5 mice in each group. Each mouse was intravenously injected with nanoparticles (2.5 mg/mL, 100 µL), and an equal volume of PBS was injected as a control. The anticoagulant blood samples were used for complete blood count 7 days after treatment. The serum samples were used for biochemical analysis involving liver and kidney function-related indexes after 3- and 7-day treatment; after that, the mice were sacrificed, and the main organs were fixed and analyzed by H&E staining.

Data were presented as mean± standard deviation (SD) with at least three independent replicates (n ≥ 3). Significant differences between groups were calculated using one-way ANOVA. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

Gd-doped carbon dots (Gd@Cdots) were prepared via pyrolytic reaction using glucose and tryptophan ( Fig. 1 a) and exhibited a diameter of 2–3 nm in TEM imaging (Fig. 1 b). The Gd@Cdots were positively charged due to their amino groups, allowing covalent and electrostatic conjugation of negatively-charged Ce6 and Ver. The addition of Ce6 and Ver resulted in self-assembly into nanoparticles with an average diameter of 40 nm (Fig. 1 c), the HRTEM image illustrates the Gd@Cdots conjugated with Ce6 and Ver within 10 nm (Fig. 1 d). XPS analysis revealed characteristic peaks corresponding to Gd 4d (141 eV), C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (398 eV), O 1s (530 eV), and Gd 3d (1187 eV), indicating a composition of 66.1% C, 21.8% O, 10.7% N, and 1.5% Gd (Fig. S1). The Gd3+ concentration was 45.95 μg/mL in a 3 mg/mL solution of Gd@Cdots (Table 1)—a Gd mass composition of 1.54%, in agreement with XPS (1.5% Gd). UV-Vis absorption peaks were observed at 340, 402, 660, and 698 nm (Fig. 1 e), and the characteristics absorption peaks of Gd@Cdots, Ce6, and Ver. The Ce6 and Ver conjugation rates were 14.59% and 32.71%, respectively, based on absorbance at 402 nm and 690 nm (Fig. S2). The zeta potential decreased from 22.1 mV for Gd@Cdots to 12.9 mV for Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver (the zeta potential of a mixture of Ce6 and Ver was −19.8 mV) (Fig. S3), confirming conjugation.

Gd@Cdots can be excited over a range of wavelengths from 340 nm to 450 nm, and have emission maxima from 440 nm to 510 nm (Fig. S4). The emission spectrum of Gd@Cdots partially overlaps with the absorbance spectrum of Ce6 and Ver nearly from 350 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 1 f), enabling FRET between Gd@Cdots and Ce6 or Ver. The absorbance spectrum of Ver and emission spectrum of free Ce6 do not overlap significantly (Fig. S5); however, the emission of Ce6 is red-shifted when Ce6 is conjugated to Gd@Cdots (Fig. S6), and overlaps with the absorbance spectrum of Ver (Fig. 1 g), creating another FRET pair and enabling cascade FRET from Gd@Cdots to Ce6 to Ver in the Gd@Cdot/Ce6/Ver nanoparticles ( Fig. 2 a). Moreover, the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver emission spectrum decreased along with the Ce6 and Ver amounts increase further illustrates that the PS was conjugated to the Gd@Cdots, and the FRET efficiency was proportional to the conjugated PS. The high FRET efficiency of 82% proved that Gd@Cdots was an excellent energy transfer donor to Ce6 and Ver (Fig. S7 , Table 3). Ce6 and Ver in the nanoparticles can be activated via different pathways depending on the PS conjugation status, including not only cascade FRET but also single FRET between Gd@Cdots and Ce6 or Ver (Fig. 2 b).

Production of 1O2 by the nanoparticles under UV irradiation was initially evaluated in the absence of cells. Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver yielded greater production of 1O2 than Gd@Cdots or Gd@Cdots/Ce6 based on a 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) assay (Fig. 2 c) [31]. The 1O2 yield under UV irradiation for 8 min was 1.6-fold greater for Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver than for Gd@Cdots (Fig. 2 d). However, 1O2 is very electrophilic and reacts readily with reducing agents in water under light irradiation. It can be easily quenched and then yield·OH in an acidic microenvironment [32]. Moreover, under UV irradiation, Gd@Cdots containing oxygen groups could form O2 -·and then changed to H2O2 and·OH. The significant improvement of relative·OH in Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver and Gd@Cdots/Ce6 groups clearly illustrated the high yield of ROS (Fig. 2 e), although the modest increase in O2 -·generation suggests that the O2 -·was unstable or its yield by Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was low (Fig. 2 f).

To determine the highest nontoxic concentration of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in the absence of UV irradiation, MUM-2B tumor cells were treated with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver at 0, 20, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL for 48 h. A Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver concentration of 50 μg/mL was the highest concentration that did not cause significant cytotoxicity (Fig. S8). Then 50 μg/mL Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was further evaluated on non-tumor cells (RPE and HUVEC) for 4 d, compared with the NC group. This did not lead to undesired cell death. Therefore, the 50 μg/mL Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver concentration was selected for subsequent experiments.

The subcellular localization of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was determined by fluorescence microscopy. Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver was found almost exclusively in the nucleus, matching the pattern of Gd@Cdots (Fig. S9a). It also exhibited the strongest fluorescence intensity among all the groups. In contrast, the single ce6 group showed little signal in the nucleus because of the low bioavailability Fig. 3 a). Meanwhile, the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver exhibited strong emissions at 475 nm and 665 nm with the excitation at 405 nm, confirming the cascade FRET effect under excitation (Fig. S9b).

To investigate the effects of these various nanomaterials on intracellular ROS production, the ROS level was first determined in ocular melanoma following UV irradiation. The Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver-treated group generated 1.52 fold higher production of intracellular ROS than the Gd@Cdots group (Fig. 3 b, c). Since 1O2 is unstable in an acidic microenvironment, and is easily reduced to H2O2 and then to·OH, the cellular·OH generation results indicate that the ROS production was more efficient in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group compared with the Gd@Cdots/Ce6 and Gd@Cdots groups (Fig. 3 d). Interestingly, the cellular O2 -·level was also significantly increased in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group compare with the Gd@Cdots/Ce6 and Gd@Cdots groups, likely because the DHR123 is more sensitive to detecting cellular O2 -·under the synergistic action of catalase (CAT) and cyto-C (Fig. 3 e) [33]. Meanwhile, the endogenic mitochondrial superoxide species in Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group also exhibited a significant enhancement (Fig. S10). Taken together, in PDT of cancer, PS-induced amounts of ROS could kill tumor cells and inhibit angiogenesis by inducing DNA damage and disrupting energy metabolism [34]. We compared the effects of PDT with different photosensitizers—Ce6, Ver, Gd@Cdots, Gd@Cdots/Ce6, and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver—on the proliferation, viability, and migration of two types of uveal melanoma cells, MUM-2B and OCM1. Cell proliferation was measured for 3 d with or without irradiation. Only Ver alone and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver significantly inhibited cell proliferation with irradiation; all other photosensitizers yielded high cell densities on the third day ( Fig. 4 a). Interestingly, Ver alone showed high cytotoxicity (inhibition of proliferation) even without irradiation. Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver had no significant effect on cell proliferation in the absence of irradiation, indicating that this PS is nontoxic until photoactivated. Cell viability was also evaluated by trypan blue staining after treatment with PS and irradiation. PDT with Ver alone and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver showed a greater reduction in cell viability than PDT with Ce6, Gd@Cdots, or Gd@Cdots/Ce6 (Fig. 4 b, Fig. S11), consistent with the cell proliferation results. PDT with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver also inhibited MUM-2B tumor cell migration, whereas PDT with Gd@Cdots and Gd@Cdots/Ce6 had a much weaker inhibitory effect (Fig. S12). The percent apoptotic MUM-2B cells increased from 9.2% in the negative control group with irradiation (NC-PDT) to 20.3% in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver PDT treatment group, using 24 h of PDT treatment (Fig. 4 c, d); a similar trend was observed when using OCM1 cells (Fig. S13). These results demonstrated that the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver act as an effective photosensitizer under UV irradiation to inhibit uveal melanoma cell growth and migration and induce cell apoptosis.

To confirm that the observed apoptosis was caused by increased production of ROS, we measured the relative expression of apoptotic and mitochondrial proteins whose expression levels are known to change under oxidative stress. Treatment with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver and irradiation induced significantly greater expression of Bax, Bcl-cl, cyto-C, Fas, caspase-3, cleaved caspase-3, and H2AX than treatment with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in the absence of irradiation ( Fig. 5 a), indicating that photoactivation of this photosensitizer triggered these changes in cell protein expression. Under UV irradiation, low expression of the mitochondrial proteins Bax and Bcl-xl and high expression of cyto C indicates loss of mitochondrial function. High levels of cyto C cause cell apoptosis, indicated by high expression of cleaved caspase-3. The enhanced expression of Fas, an apoptosis-related member receptor, indicates greater susceptibility of the tumor cells to activated T cells. Expression of the DNA damage-related protein H2AX was greater when using PDT with Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver than with Gd@Cdots/Ce6 or Gd@Cdots (Fig. 5 b–c). Together, these results are consistent with the induction of tumor cell apoptosis via three pathways (Fig. 5 d): i) oxidative damage to tumor cell nuclear DNA; ii) an immune response triggered by increased Fas; and iii) the caspase-3 apoptosis pathway indicated by the release of cyto C from damaged mitochondria.

To further evaluate the therapeutic efficiency of PDT with the nano PS, subcutaneous and in situ uveal melanoma in vivo models were used ( Fig. 6 a). The tumor targeting ability and biodistribution of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver were evaluated in subcutaneous models over a period of 24 h. IVIS imaging revealed fluorescent signals from Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in the tumor site at 4 h after IV injection, which remained in the tumor site until 24 h after IV injection. Fluorescent signals in the liver and kidneys in ex vivo imaging indicated that the PS was metabolized through the liver and kidneys (Fig. 6 b). The ICP results documented a higher distribution of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver in the tumor tissue than Gd@Cdots/Ce6 and Gd@Cdots. The liver was the main tissue for metabolism, likely by the Kupffer cells [35], and metabolism by the kidney appeared minimal (Fig. S14). Since the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver has good relaxivity, with an r1 value of 12.8 mM−1 s−1 (Fig. S15 ) and a detection sensitivity comparable to the clinical MRI contrast agent (Fig. S16), its biodistribution in mice could be observed by T1-weighted MR imaging after injection. For Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver and Gd@Cdots/Ce6 PSs, the T1 signal in both sagittal and coronal axes increased significantly after injection 4 h and 24 h; in contrast, there was no difference in the Ce6 group until 24 h (Fig. S17). Together, the IVIS and MRI results showed that the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver have good tumor-targeting ability.

The tumor volume after 16 d was significantly lower in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group (484.67 ± 129.06 mm3) than in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6 group (933.50 ± 42.5 mm3) and Gd@Cdots group (1406.67 ± 93.33 mm3) (Fig. 6 c). Similarly, after the mice were sacrificed, the ex vivo tumor weight was significantly lower in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group (468.33 ± 128.50 mg) than in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6 group (846.67 ± 121.47 mg) and Gd@Cdots group (1196.67 ± 101.27 mg) (Fig. 6 d). These results indicate that the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver has a greater inhibitory effect on uveal melanoma tumor growth than the other PS tested.

To further evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of PDT with different photosensitizers, bioluminescent luciferase-expressing cells were injected into the vitreous cavity of the mice to construct an in situ uveal melanoma tumor model. The bioluminescent signals in the uveal melanoma matched the fluorescent signals of the PSs in the intraocular tumor during the five weeks of weekly IV injection (Fig. 6 e). Region of interest (ROI) bioluminescent signals in the ocular tumors (Fig. 6 f) and tumor eye weight (Fig. 6 h) both indicated a lower tumor growth rate in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group than in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6 and Gd@Cdots groups. In vivo images showed that the bioluminescence at week 5 was the weakest in the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver treatment group (Fig. S18). H&E staining indicated that the whole ocular tissue was severely infiltrated by uveal melanoma and the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group showed an anti-tumor immune inflammation after PDT treatment. Moreover, the abundant TUNEL signals in the tumor tissue indicated the efficient cell kill of the Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver group (Fig. 6 g). These in vivo results demonstrate that the photosensitizing Gd@Cdot/Ce6/Ver nanoparticles exhibit both specific tumor targeting and tumor growth inhibition in PDT of uveal melanoma.

To further evaluate the biosafety of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver, the biochemical indexes including albumin (ALB), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and urea were measured at days 3 and 7 post-treatment. Compared with the PBS group, both Gd@Cdots and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver exhibited normal liver and kidney functions (Fig. S19a-d). Whole blood samples and tissues were harvested 7 days after treatment for complete blood counting and H&E staining. There was no statistically significant difference in total blood count between the PBS group and the nanoparticle treatment groups (Fig. S19e). In addition, the H&E staining of the lung, liver, spleen, kidney, and heart showed no recognizable changes in the Gd@Cdots and Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver groups compared to the PBS group (Fig. S19f), which further demonstrated the biosafety of Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver.

Together, these in vitro and in vivo results demonstrate that the Gd@Cdot/Ce6/Ver nanoparticles serve as an effective photosensitizer for PDT of uveal melanoma due to their enhanced, multi-pathway generation of cytotoxic 1O2 and ROS, which results in increased potency of killing tumor cells and enhanced tumor growth inhibition. These Gd@Cdots/Ce6/Ver could also be used as a dual-modal contrast agent for both fluorescence imaging and MRI. This Gd@Cdot -based photosensitizer system shows the effectiveness of a cascade FRET-based strategy to increase 1O2 and ROS production in PDT via multiple photoactivation pathways, and may be useful for improving the clinical therapeutic efficacy of PDT for uveal melanoma.

Ruan Jing: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original draft preparation, Funding acquisition, Visualization, Li Fang: Methodology, Writing-Original draft preparation, Visualization, Tian Hao: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Yu Jie: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Deng Hongpei: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Ge Shengfang: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Kam W Leong: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Validation.
